algorithms.name: BFS (Breadth-First Search)
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(V) · for the visited set and queue
algorithms.analogy: Ripples in a pond — you explore all neighbors one hop away before going two hops.
algorithms.story: Imagine you're in a maze. Instead of running down one corridor hoping it leads out, you send scouts one step in every direction simultaneously. Each scout spawns more scouts. The first one to reach the exit wins — and you know that's the shortest path because all scouts move at the same speed.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Find shortest path in an unweighted graph
• Level-order traversal (tree or graph)
• Check if a graph is bipartite
• Find all connected components
• Word ladder / transformation sequence problems
• Minimum steps / fewest operations problems
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Add the start node to a Queue and mark it visited
2. While the queue is not empty:
3.   Dequeue the front node — this is the current node
4.   For each unvisited neighbor of current:
5.     Mark neighbor as visited
6.     Enqueue neighbor
7. Each iteration processes one 'level' of the graph
8. Track parent pointers if you need to reconstruct the path
algorithms.interviewTips:
• BFS guarantees shortest path only in unweighted graphs — for weighted use Dijkstra
• Always use a visited set BEFORE enqueueing (not after dequeuing) to avoid duplicate enqueues
• Level-order: track queue.size() at the start of each while iteration to process level by level
• Bidirectional BFS cuts search space from O(b^d) to O(b^(d/2)) — mention this for large graphs
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Social Network Degrees","desc":"LinkedIn's 'degrees of connection' uses BFS — 1st, 2nd, 3rd degree connections are just BFS levels from your node."},{"title":"GPS Navigation","desc":"Routing apps use BFS (or Dijkstra for weighted roads) to find the fewest-turn route between two points."},{"title":"Web Crawler","desc":"Google's original PageRank crawler used BFS starting from seed URLs, discovering linked pages level by level."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Not marking nodes visited before enqueue — causes exponential duplicate processing","Using DFS when shortest path is required — DFS finds A path, not THE shortest path","Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs — run BFS from every unvisited node","Using BFS on a weighted graph expecting shortest path — weight 1 edge ≠ all edges"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"0-1 BFS","desc":"Use a deque — push to front for weight-0 edges, back for weight-1 edges — gives shortest path in O(V+E)"},{"name":"Multi-source BFS","desc":"Enqueue all source nodes simultaneously — finds shortest distance from any source (e.g. nearest 0 in a grid)"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["Start: Enqueue source, mark visited"] --> B{"Queue empty?"}
    B -- No --> C["Dequeue front node"]
    C --> D["For each unvisited neighbor"]
    D --> E["Mark neighbor visited"]
    E --> F["Enqueue neighbor"]
    F --> B
    B -- Yes --> G["Done — all reachable nodes visited"]
algorithms.keyInsight: BFS guarantees shortest path ONLY in unweighted graphs. Mark nodes visited BEFORE enqueueing — not after dequeuing — to prevent the same node being enqueued multiple times causing exponential work. Level-order separation requires snapshotting queue.size() at the start of each while loop iteration.
algorithms.patternSignal: shortest path unweighted, minimum steps, fewest operations, level order traversal, word ladder, bipartite check, connected components, multi-source BFS, 0-1 BFS
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Word Ladder","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Each word is a node; two words are connected if they differ by exactly one letter. BFS gives the shortest transformation sequence"},{"name":"Rotting Oranges","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Multi-source BFS: enqueue ALL rotten oranges at start, BFS spreads infection level by level"},{"name":"01 Matrix (Distance to Nearest 0)","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Multi-source BFS from all 0 cells simultaneously — each cell's distance is the BFS level when first reached"},{"name":"Course Schedule (Kahn's BFS)","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Topological sort via BFS — if result size < n courses, there is a cycle"},{"name":"Number of Islands","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"BFS from each unvisited '1', marking all connected '1's visited — count how many BFS calls you make"}]

algorithms.name: DFS (Depth-First Search)
algorithms.analogy: Exploring a cave — always go deeper until you hit a dead end, then backtrack to the last fork.
algorithms.story: Theseus enters the Minotaur's labyrinth with a ball of string. He always walks as deep as possible, unrolling string behind him. When he hits a dead end, he follows the string back to the last unexplored fork. DFS is Theseus — depth before breadth, always.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Detect cycles in a graph
• Topological sort (directed acyclic graph)
• Find all paths between two nodes
• Connected components (undirected graph)
• Strongly connected components (Kosaraju/Tarjan)
• Maze/puzzle solving where any valid solution is acceptable
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Push start node on stack (or call recursively), mark visited
2. Pop current node
3. For each unvisited neighbor of current:
4.   Mark neighbor visited
5.   Push neighbor (or recurse)
6. Repeat until stack is empty
7. For cycle detection: track nodes in current recursion stack (gray/white/black coloring)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• DFS on directed graphs: use three colors (white/gray/black) to distinguish unvisited, in-stack, and done
• Topological sort post-order: push node to stack AFTER recursing all neighbors — then reverse
• For large graphs, iterative DFS avoids stack overflow (Java default stack ~500 frames)
• DFS finds a path, not necessarily the shortest — clarify which is needed
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Compiler Dependency Resolution","desc":"Build tools (Maven, Gradle) use DFS topological sort to determine which modules to compile first when there are dependencies."},{"title":"Maze Generation","desc":"Recursive backtracking (DFS) generates perfect mazes — it carves passages deeper and deeper until it must backtrack."},{"title":"Git Ancestry","desc":"git log follows the commit DAG using DFS — each commit points to its parent, and DFS reconstructs the full history."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Using a visited set that persists across DFS calls — for cycle detection in directed graphs you need per-path tracking (gray nodes), not just global visited","Topological sort on graphs with cycles — always check for cycles first","Recursive DFS on deep graphs causing StackOverflowError — use iterative DFS with an explicit stack for production","Confusing DFS order: pre-order adds node on entry, post-order adds node on exit — topological sort needs post-order"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Iterative DFS","desc":"Replace recursion with an explicit Stack<Integer> — avoids stack overflow on deep graphs"},{"name":"Kosaraju's SCC","desc":"Two-pass DFS: first pass gets finish order, second pass on reversed graph finds strongly connected components"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["Start: Push source, mark visited"] --> B{"Stack empty?"}
    B -- No --> C["Pop current node"]
    C --> D{"Has unvisited neighbor?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Mark neighbor visited"]
    E --> F["Push neighbor"]
    F --> B
    D -- No --> B
    B -- Yes --> G["Done"]
algorithms.keyInsight: DFS on directed graphs needs THREE colors: WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (currently in stack), BLACK (finished). Hitting a GRAY node = back edge = cycle. Topological sort = DFS post-order: push to stack AFTER all neighbors are processed, then reverse the stack. For undirected graphs, just track parent to avoid treating the edge you came from as a cycle.
algorithms.patternSignal: detect cycle directed graph, topological sort, all paths between nodes, strongly connected components, flood fill, DFS post-order, iterative DFS explicit stack, connected components
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Course Schedule (DFS cycle detection)","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"3-color DFS: GRAY = in current DFS path. If you visit a GRAY neighbor, there's a cycle"},{"name":"All Paths From Source to Target","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"DFS backtracking: at each node add to path, recurse all neighbors, remove from path after"},{"name":"Pacific Atlantic Water Flow","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"DFS inward from both oceans — cells reachable from both sets are the answer"},{"name":"Number of Islands","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"DFS from each unvisited '1', mark all connected cells visited — count DFS calls"},{"name":"Clone Graph","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"DFS with HashMap node→clone. If clone exists return it; else create and recurse neighbors"}]

algorithms.name: Dijkstra's Shortest Path
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(V) · for dist[] and priority queue
algorithms.analogy: GPS navigation — always expand the nearest unvisited city, updating distances to its neighbors.
algorithms.story: Edsger Dijkstra invented this algorithm in 20 minutes in a café in 1956. He wanted to show that computers could find routes. The insight: always commit to the shortest known path first. Like a greedy hiker who always takes the closest next peak — guaranteed to find the shortest total route in any non-negative weighted graph.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Shortest path in a weighted graph with non-negative edges
• Network routing (OSPF uses Dijkstra)
• GPS navigation
• Any 'minimum cost to reach X' problem with non-negative weights
• Do NOT use if graph has negative edge weights (use Bellman-Ford instead)
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Initialize dist[src] = 0, dist[all others] = ∞
2. Add (0, src) to a min-heap (priority queue)
3. While heap is not empty:
4.   Extract node with minimum distance → (d, node)
5.   If d > dist[node], skip (stale entry)
6.   For each neighbor with edge weight w:
7.     newDist = dist[node] + w
8.     If newDist < dist[neighbor]:
9.       dist[neighbor] = newDist
10.      Push (newDist, neighbor) to heap
11. dist[] now holds shortest path from src to every node
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Skip stale entries: when you pop (d, node) from heap, check if d > dist[node] — if so, skip
• Dijkstra fails with negative weights — Bellman-Ford handles negatives in O(VE)
• For path reconstruction: maintain a parent[] array alongside dist[]
• If all edge weights are 1, plain BFS is faster (O(V+E) vs O((V+E) log V))
• Modified Dijkstra on a grid: treat each cell as a node, 4-directional neighbors
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Network Routing (OSPF)","desc":"Internet routers run Dijkstra on their network topology to build routing tables — every 'shortest path' your packet takes is Dijkstra."},{"title":"Flight Path Optimization","desc":"Airline booking systems find cheapest multi-stop routes using Dijkstra on a graph where edges are flight costs."},{"title":"Game AI Pathfinding","desc":"A* (used in games and maps) is Dijkstra with a heuristic — understanding Dijkstra is the prerequisite to A*."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Using Dijkstra with negative weights — produces wrong answers; use Bellman-Ford instead","Not skipping stale heap entries — a node can be in the heap multiple times; only process if d == dist[node]","Integer overflow: dist[node] + weight can overflow int when dist is MAX_VALUE — guard with a check before adding","Undirected vs directed: for undirected, add edges in both directions"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"A* Search","desc":"Dijkstra + heuristic function h(node) — prioritizes nodes closer to the goal, much faster in practice for single-target search"},{"name":"Bellman-Ford","desc":"O(VE) — slower but handles negative edges and detects negative cycles"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["Init: dist[src]=0, dist[rest]=∞"] --> B["Push (0, src) to min-heap"]
    B --> C{"Heap empty?"}
    C -- No --> D["Pop (d, node) with min distance"]
    D --> E{"d > dist[node]?"}
    E -- Yes --> C
    E -- No --> F["For each neighbor with weight w"]
    F --> G{"dist[node]+w < dist[neighbor]?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Update dist[neighbor]"]
    H --> I["Push (newDist, neighbor) to heap"]
    I --> F
    G -- No --> F
    C -- Yes --> J["Done — dist[] has all shortest paths"]
algorithms.keyInsight: Two rules to avoid bugs: (1) Skip stale heap entries — if d > dist[node], continue. (2) Never use Dijkstra with negative weights. If all edge weights are 1, use BFS instead (O(V+E) vs O((V+E) log V)). For path reconstruction, maintain a parent[] array alongside dist[].
algorithms.patternSignal: shortest path weighted graph, non-negative edges, minimum cost to reach, network delay, minimum weight path, GPS routing, priority queue shortest path, path with minimum effort
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Network Delay Time","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Single-source Dijkstra from node k — answer is max of all dist[] values, -1 if any node is unreachable"},{"name":"Cheapest Flights Within K Stops","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Modified Dijkstra or Bellman-Ford with K+1 relaxation rounds — use a copy of dist[] each round"},{"name":"Path with Minimum Effort","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Dijkstra on grid: edge weight = absolute height difference. Minimize maximum edge weight on path"},{"name":"Swim in Rising Water","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Dijkstra where dist = max grid value on path, or binary search on answer + BFS validation"}]

algorithms.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set)
algorithms.analogy: Friend circles — two people are in the same group if following 'who introduced you' chains leads to the same root person.
algorithms.story: It's orientation week. 1000 students arrive one by one. Each time two students meet, their friend circles merge. If you want to know later whether Alice and Bob are in the same circle, you just trace back who each circle's 'representative' is. Union-Find makes each merge and each check nearly O(1) with two tricks: path compression and union by rank.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Find connected components in an undirected graph
• Detect cycles in undirected graphs
• Kruskal's Minimum Spanning Tree algorithm
• Dynamic connectivity queries
• Grouping / merging problems (accounts merge, number of islands II)
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Initialize: parent[i] = i, rank[i] = 0 for all nodes
2. find(x): follow parent pointers until parent[x] == x (the root)
   • Path compression: make every node on the path point directly to the root
3. union(x, y): find roots of x and y
   • If same root, already connected (cycle if adding an edge)
   • If different, attach the tree with lower rank under the higher-rank root
   • If same rank, attach either and increment winner's rank
4. connected(x, y): find(x) == find(y)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Both path compression AND union by rank are needed for O(α(n)) — either alone is only O(log n)
• α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function — effectively constant (≤ 4) for any realistic n
• union() returning false means x and y were already connected — use this to detect cycles
• For Kruskal's MST: sort edges by weight, add each if union() returns true (not a cycle)
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Kruskal's Minimum Spanning Tree","desc":"Sort all edges by weight, use Union-Find to greedily add edges without creating cycles — builds the cheapest connected network."},{"title":"LeetCode Accounts Merge","desc":"Each account email is a node; union emails that share an account — find() reveals which master account each email belongs to."},{"title":"Percolation (Physics Simulation)","desc":"Simulate water flowing through a porous material — union-find checks if the top and bottom are connected through open cells."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting path compression — find() stays O(log n) worst case without it","Applying Union-Find to directed graphs — it's designed for undirected; directed graphs need DFS for cycle detection","Off-by-one in grid problems: mapping (r, c) to 1D index as r*cols + c","Counting components naively — maintain a counter and decrement it in union() for O(1) count queries"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Weighted Union-Find","desc":"Track the weight/offset between nodes for problems like 'difference constraints' or relative rankings"},{"name":"Rollback Union-Find","desc":"Use union by rank without path compression to support undo operations — needed for offline dynamic connectivity"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["find(x): Is parent[x] == x?"] -- Yes --> B["Return x (this is the root)"]
    A -- No --> C["parent[x] = find(parent[x])
Path compression!"]
    C --> B
    D["union(x, y)"] --> E["rx = find(x), ry = find(y)"]
    E --> F{"rx == ry?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Already connected — return false"]
    F -- No --> H{"Compare rank"}
    H --> I["Attach lower-rank root under higher"]
    I --> J["components--"] --> K["Return true"]
algorithms.keyInsight: Path compression + union by rank are BOTH required for O(α(n)) — either alone gives O(log n). The power move: union() returning false means the two nodes were already connected — use this to detect cycles in undirected graphs or identify redundant edges in Kruskal's MST.
algorithms.patternSignal: connected components, detect cycle undirected, merge groups, number of islands, accounts merge, redundant connection, Kruskal MST, dynamic connectivity, same component check
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Number of Islands","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Union adjacent '1' cells. Components counter starts at island count and decrements on each successful union"},{"name":"Redundant Connection","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Add edges one by one — the first edge where union() returns false (already connected) is redundant"},{"name":"Accounts Merge","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Each email is a node. Union emails in the same account. find() groups them by root representative"},{"name":"Number of Connected Components in Undirected Graph","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Initialize with n components, decrement on each successful union() — final count is the answer"},{"name":"Satisfiability of Equality Equations","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Union all == pairs first, then check if any != pair shares the same find() root — contradiction = false"}]

algorithms.name: Topological Sort
algorithms.analogy: Getting dressed — you must put on your socks before your shoes, pants before your belt. Topological sort finds a valid order when there are dependency constraints.
algorithms.story: A build system needs to compile 50 Java files. File A imports B and C; B imports D. If you compile in random order, you'll get 'class not found' errors. Topological sort finds a compilation order that satisfies all dependencies — it's the algorithm that makes 'mvn compile' work.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Task scheduling with dependencies
• Build system (compile order)
• Course prerequisite ordering
• Detecting cycles in directed graphs
• Any problem where elements must be ordered by dependency
algorithms.howItWorks:
Kahn's Algorithm (BFS-based):
1. Compute in-degree for every node
2. Enqueue all nodes with in-degree 0 (no prerequisites)
3. While queue not empty:
4.   Dequeue node, add to result
5.   For each neighbor: decrement its in-degree
6.   If neighbor's in-degree becomes 0, enqueue it
7. If result.size() < n → cycle exists (some nodes were never added)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Kahn's detects cycles naturally: if result.size() < n, there's a cycle (some node never hit in-degree 0)
• DFS post-order also gives topological sort — push to stack after recursing all neighbors, then reverse
• If multiple valid orderings exist, use a min-heap instead of queue for lexicographically smallest ordering
• 'Course Schedule II' (LeetCode 210) is the direct application — memorize this pattern
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Build Systems","desc":"Maven/Gradle compute module dependency graphs and topological sort to determine which JARs to compile and package first."},{"title":"Spreadsheet Evaluation","desc":"Excel evaluates cell formulas in topological order — a cell with =A1+B1 is evaluated after A1 and B1 are computed."},{"title":"Package Managers","desc":"npm/pip install packages in topological order of their dependency graph to avoid 'module not found' during installation."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Applying topological sort to undirected graphs — it's only defined for Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)","Not detecting cycles — always check result.size() == n","Confusing in-degree (prerequisites) with out-degree","For DFS approach: pushing to result on ENTRY (pre-order) instead of EXIT (post-order) gives wrong order"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"DFS Post-order","desc":"Recursive DFS — push each node to a stack after all its neighbors are processed, then pop for the sorted order"},{"name":"Lexicographic Topo Sort","desc":"Use a min-heap (PriorityQueue) instead of a plain queue to get the lexicographically smallest valid order"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["Compute in-degree for all nodes"] --> B["Enqueue nodes with in-degree 0"]
    B --> C{"Queue empty?"}
    C -- No --> D["Dequeue node, add to result"]
    D --> E["For each neighbor: decrement in-degree"]
    E --> F{"neighbor in-degree == 0?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Enqueue neighbor"]
    G --> C
    F -- No --> C
    C -- Yes --> H{"result.size == n?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Valid topological order!"]
    H -- No --> J["Cycle detected!"]
algorithms.keyInsight: Kahn's algorithm is the one to master: in-degree 0 = no prerequisites = ready to process. result.size() < n at the end means a cycle exists — some nodes never reached in-degree 0. This detects cycles automatically without a separate DFS cycle check.
algorithms.patternSignal: task scheduling with dependencies, course prerequisites, build order, compile order, dependency resolution, DAG ordering, detect cycle directed graph, sequence with constraints
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Course Schedule","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Kahn's: if topo sort processes all n courses, no cycle — return true. result.size() < n = cycle"},{"name":"Course Schedule II","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Return the actual topological order list from Kahn's — that list IS the valid course order"},{"name":"Alien Dictionary","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Build character dependency graph from adjacent words in the wordlist, topological sort gives character order"},{"name":"Minimum Height Trees","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Topologically peel leaves (degree 1) inward — last 1-2 remaining nodes are the MST roots"},{"name":"Parallel Courses","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Topo sort BFS levels — answer is number of BFS levels (semesters) needed to complete all courses"}]
